cards.prompt: ThreadLocal<T> gives each thread its own copy: main and worker both read 100 from the factory, and worker's write of 999 is invisible to main
cards.code: using System; using System.Threading; var tl = new ThreadLocal<int>(() => 100); int mainVal = tl.Value; int workerVal = 0; var t = new Thread(() => { workerVal = tl.Value; tl.Value = 999; }); t.Start(); t.Join(); int mainAfter = tl.Value; Console.WriteLine($"main={mainVal} worker={workerVal} mainAfter={mainAfter}");

cards.prompt: ThreadLocal<T> initializes lazily: IsValueCreated is False before the first Value access, the factory yields 42 on first access, then IsValueCreated is True
cards.code: using System; using System.Threading; var tl = new ThreadLocal<int>(() => 42); bool before = tl.IsValueCreated; int v = tl.Value; bool after = tl.IsValueCreated; Console.WriteLine($"before={before} value={v} after={after}");

cards.prompt: AsyncLocal<T> is ambient to the async control flow: a value set before an await is still observable after the await, even when the continuation runs on another thread
cards.code: using System; using System.Threading; using System.Threading.Tasks; var al = new AsyncLocal<string>(); al.Value = "ctx"; string beforeAwait = al.Value; async Task<string> Inner() { await Task.Delay(10); return al.Value; } string afterAwait = Inner().GetAwaiter().GetResult(); Console.WriteLine($"before={beforeAwait} after={afterAwait}");
